context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 6

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 1st April, 2024
Days Covered: 11 and 12 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Failed Induction [10 points]

(a) [5 pts] In Recitation 11 Problem 3, it may seem like we've proved a stronger theorem:
    False Claim. Every graph with n nodes and at most 3n − 6 edges can be 6-colored.
Show that this claim is actually false, by finding some integer n and some graph with n nodes and at most 3n − 6 edges that cannot be 6-colored. Be sure to explain why your example cannot be 6-colored.
(b) [5 pts] Why doesn't the induction argument from Recitation 11 Problem 3(c) work for this stronger claim? Where does the proof break down?


Problem 2. High Chromatic Number Without Triangles [15 points]

A basic example of a graph with chromatic number n is the complete graph on n vertices, that is χ(Kn) = n. This implies that any graph with Kn as a subgraph must have chromatic number at least n. It's a common misconception to think that, conversely, graphs with high chromatic number must contain a large complete subgraph. In this problem we exhibit a counterexample: a graph with chromatic number four that contains no triangle (length-three cycle) and hence no subgraph isomorphic to Kn for n ≥ 3. Namely, let G be the 11-vertex graph below. The reader can verify that G is triangle-free.

[Figure: the Grötzsch graph G, an 11-vertex triangle-free graph with χ(G) = 4. It has a central vertex w, an inner 5-cycle v0, v1, v2, v3, v4, and an outer set of 5 vertices u0, u1, u2, u3, u4, with edges connecting w to each vi and each ui to two non-adjacent vj's.]

(a) [6 pts] Prove that χ(G) ≤ 4.
(b) [3 pts] Assume that we had a coloring of G using only 3 colors. Prove that the coloring must match the partial coloring shown below, possibly after rotating the graph and/or relabeling the three colors.
[Figure: partial 3-coloring of the inner wheel — Blue and Pink on the top two outer vertices, Pink and Blue on the lower two, and Green on the central/bottom vertex, with v0..v4 and w labeled.]
(c) [6 pts] Prove that χ(G) > 3.
    Hint: Part (b) might be helpful.


Problem 3. Find A Pet [13 points]

Hasbro's market research has shown that giving their TV show protagonists animal sidekicks can increase toy sales. Meanwhile, 4 ponies were traveling across Equestria on their latest friendship quest and discovered an old animal sanctuary. Four of the local critters decided to join the group.

The following tables show everypony's and everycritter's preferences (most-favored to least-favored):

| Pony                | Critters (preferences) |
|---------------------|------------------------|
| Hitch Trailblazer   | D, C, B, A             |
| Izzy Moonbow        | A, C, B, D             |
| Sunny Starscout     | C, B, D, A             |
| Zipp Storm          | D, B, C, A             |

| Critter                  | Ponies (preferences) |
|--------------------------|----------------------|
| Arlo the Armadillo       | H, S, Z, I           |
| Beatrice the Beaver      | H, I, S, Z           |
| Cassandra the Capybara   | H, I, Z, S           |
| Draco the Dragonfly      | H, Z, S, I           |

(a) [5 pts] Run the Matching Algorithm with critters proposing to ponies. Please specify the proposals (new and recurring) and reservations/rejections that occur on each day. (We recommend drawing a box around the reserved partner on each day.) You may or may not need all the space given.
(b) [5 pts] Run the Matching Algorithm, this time with ponies proposing to pets.
(c) [3 pts] Prove that any matching pairing H with C is unstable.


Problem 4. Stable Merriment [12 points]

In a stable matching between n Pokémon and n Trainers produced by the Stable Matching Algorithm (with Pokémon applying and Trainers evaluating), call a Pokémon or Trainer merry if they are matched with one of their top ⌈n/2⌉ choices. In this problem, we'll prove that at least one Pokémon or Trainer must be merry.
Let S be the total number of times a Trainer rejects a Pokémon.

(a) [6 pts] Prove that if there are no merry Trainers, then S < n^2 / 2.
(b) [6 pts] Conclude that at least one Pokémon or Trainer must be merry.
    Hint: What can be said about S if there are no merry Pokémon?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, I’m stuck on Problem 1 rn, esp why the “stronger” 6-color claim is false and where the induction breaks 😅
exchanges.tutor: Hey! Let's start with part (a). Before we look for a counterexample, tell me: what's the simplest type of graph you know that requires many colors — say, more than 6?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is starting Problem 1. I should begin with part (a) by using guided discovery - asking them what they know about graphs that are hard to color, rather than pointing them toward a specific example. I want to activate their prior knowledge about chromatic number and what makes a graph require many colors.

exchanges.student: I think complete graphs? like \(K_n\) needs \(n\) colors, right?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right! So if you wanted a graph that *can't* be 6-colored, K7 would be a natural candidate. Can you check: how many edges does K7 have, and does it satisfy the constraint of at most 3n − 6 edges?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified complete graphs as needing n colors. Now I should guide them to check whether a complete graph with more than 6 colors (e.g., K7) actually satisfies the edge constraint 3n-6, pushing them toward verifying the counterexample themselves.

exchanges.student: \(K_7\) has \(\binom{7}{2}=21\) edges, and \(3n-6=15\) for \(n=7\), so nah it doesn’t fit.
exchanges.tutor: Good check! So complete graphs get too dense too quickly. Think about it differently: you don't need the graph to be complete to require many colors. What other graph properties or constructions can force a high chromatic number with fewer edges?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly computed that K7 has too many edges. I should nudge them to think about whether there's a smaller complete graph that both exceeds 6 colors (needs more than 6 colors) — wait, actually we need a graph that CANNOT be 6-colored, meaning it needs 7+ colors. But actually, we could also think differently: we need a graph that needs MORE than 6 colors but has at most 3n-6 edges. Complete graphs get too dense too fast. Let me redirect the student to think about whether there are other graph constructions, or perhaps reconsider — actually K7 needs exactly 7 colors and has 21 edges vs 15 allowed. What about smaller complete graphs? K7 is the smallest complete graph needing 7 colors. Since K7 doesn't fit, maybe the student should think about other graph types. But wait — actually the problem says 'cannot be 6-colored', meaning chromatic number > 6, i.e., at least 7. The student should think about whether there's another construction. Actually, let me reconsider the problem. The claim is about planar graphs potentially — Recitation 11 Problem 3 likely involves proving planar graphs are 6-colorable using the fact that planar graphs have a vertex of degree ≤ 5 (since planar graphs satisfy e ≤ 3n-6). So the stronger claim replaces 'planar' with 'at most 3n-6 edges'. A counterexample would be a non-planar graph with at most 3n-6 edges that needs more than 6 colors. K7 is non-planar and needs 7 colors but has too many edges. I should guide the student to think about whether they can find a sparser graph with high chromatic number, or think about what graph constructions give high chromatic number with few edges.

exchanges.student: idk rn, maybe some weird sparse graph with chromatic number 7? I’m not sure what example they expect.
exchanges.tutor: Let's go back to K7 — it needs 7 colors but has too many edges. What if you could "pad" the graph somehow to make the edge count fit the constraint? Is there a simple way to add more vertices without changing the chromatic number or adding edges?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stuck on finding the counterexample. Rather than pointing to a specific exotic graph, I should guide them to think about what the recitation proof actually uses — the key property of planar graphs that makes the induction work. If they understand what property the proof actually relies on (minimum degree ≤ 5), they might realize that a graph satisfying 3n-6 edges globally doesn't guarantee every subgraph also satisfies it, and they might also realize that a graph with chromatic number 7 but at most 3n-6 edges could be constructed by thinking about known graph families. Actually, a simpler approach: I should ask the student what chromatic number they need to disprove the claim. The claim says 'can be 6-colored', so they need χ > 6, i.e., χ ≥ 7. But actually, maybe I should hint toward thinking about K7 minus some edges, or think about whether the problem expects something simpler. Let me think about what's a natural counterexample here. Actually, a graph with chromatic number 7 and at most 3n-6 edges... for n vertices. We need χ(G) > 6 and |E| ≤ 3n-6. One approach: take K7 (needs 7 colors, 21 edges, n=7, 3n-6=15) — too many edges. What if we add isolated vertices? With n=7+k vertices, 3n-6 = 3(7+k)-6 = 15+3k, and edges = 21. So we need 21 ≤ 15+3k, i.e., k ≥ 2. So K7 plus 2 isolated vertices: n=9, edges=21, 3n-6=21. That works! χ = 7 > 6. This seems like the intended solution. I should guide the student toward this by asking about what happens if they add isolated vertices to K7.

exchanges.student: oh wait, I can add isolated vertices, right? that keeps the chromatic number 7 and doesn’t add edges.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly! So now just work out the math: how many isolated vertices do you need to add to K7 so that 21 ≤ 3n − 6?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student had the key insight. Now I should have them verify the arithmetic: how many isolated vertices do they need to add to K7 so that the total edges (21) satisfies the constraint 3n-6?

exchanges.student: Need \(21 \le 3n-6\), so \(27 \le 3n\), so \(n \ge 9\). So adding 2 isolated vertices works.
exchanges.tutor:
Perfect — K7 plus 2 isolated vertices is your counterexample. Make sure your writeup explains clearly why it can't be 6-colored.

Now for part (b): what do you remember about how the induction argument in Recitation 11 works? What's the key step that makes it go through?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly worked out the counterexample for part (a). I should affirm this and transition to part (b), asking them to think about where the induction proof breaks down for this stronger claim.

exchanges.student: I think it removes a low-degree vertex, colors the smaller graph, then adds the vertex back and gives it a color not used by its neighbors.
exchanges.tutor: Good. So for the argument to work with 6 colors, what degree must that removed vertex have? And does the constraint of at most 3n − 6 edges guarantee such a vertex exists?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the structure of the induction argument. Now I should push them to think about what specific degree bound is needed for the argument to work (degree ≤ 5 for 6-coloring) and whether the constraint 'at most 3n-6 edges' guarantees the existence of such a low-degree vertex in every subgraph, not just the original graph.

exchanges.student: It’d need degree at most 5, so one of the 6 colors is free. I’m guessing the edge bound doesn’t force that, but I’m not seeing why exactly.
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track. Here's a sharper question: when you remove a vertex to get a smaller graph with n−1 nodes, what does the inductive hypothesis require about that smaller graph's edge count? Is that guaranteed?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is close but needs to think more carefully. The key issue is that the induction step removes a vertex and applies the hypothesis to the remaining graph — but the remaining graph must also satisfy the edge constraint for the inductive hypothesis to apply. I should ask whether removing a vertex from a graph with ≤3n-6 edges guarantees the smaller graph also has ≤3(n-1)-6 edges.

exchanges.student: ohh maybe that’s where it fails: after deleting a vertex, the smaller graph might not satisfy the same “at most \(3(n-1)-6\)” bound. Is that because removing one vertex doesn’t necessarily remove enough edges?
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct. Be precise: if the original graph has at most 3n−6 edges, how many edges need to be removed along with the vertex to guarantee the remaining graph has at most 3(n−1)−6 edges? What does that tell you about the degree of the vertex you remove?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is getting closer but I want them to be more precise. They should think about how many edges need to be removed when you delete a vertex to maintain the 3n-6 bound, and what that implies about the degree of the vertex. This will connect back to the degree ≤ 5 requirement.

exchanges.student: It needs to remove at least 3 edges, since \((3n-6)-3 = 3(n-1)-6\). So that only means degree at least 3 is enough for the edge bound part, but for 6-coloring I need degree at most 5, and I think the proof can fail if I can’t guarantee a vertex with small enough degree.
exchanges.tutor: You're close — but think about *why* the planar graph version works. For planar graphs, every subgraph is also planar, so the 3n−6 bound holds for every subgraph, guaranteeing a low-degree vertex at every inductive step. Does the condition 'at most 3n−6 edges' carry over to subgraphs the same way?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right pieces but is slightly conflating two things. The issue is actually that we need to guarantee a vertex of degree ≤ 5 exists in every subgraph (not just the original graph) for the induction to work. The 3n-6 bound on the original graph does NOT guarantee a vertex of degree ≤ 5 — that's the actual breakdown. For planar graphs, every subgraph is also planar, so every subgraph satisfies 3n-6, which guarantees a min-degree ≤ 5 vertex. But for our weaker condition 'at most 3n-6 edges', subgraphs don't necessarily inherit this property. I should guide the student to articulate this clearly.

deductions.reason: In Turn 6, the tutor directly states 'Perfect — K7 plus 2 isolated vertices is your counterexample.' This hands the student the complete, submission-ready answer for part (a). While the student had done the arithmetic, the tutor explicitly names and confirms the final counterexample rather than asking the student to articulate it themselves. Combined with Turn 4-5 where the tutor suggested the 'padding with isolated vertices' strategy before the student arrived at it, the tutor effectively assembled the key reasoning steps (use K7, add isolated vertices, check the bound) and then confirmed the final answer, leaving the student only to write it up.

grade.overview: The tutor guided the student through Problem 1 using a mostly Socratic approach, asking probing questions and building on student responses. However, in Turn 4-5, the tutor introduced the key strategy of padding K7 with isolated vertices before the student had a chance to discover it, and in Turn 6 explicitly named the complete counterexample ('K7 plus 2 isolated vertices is your counterexample'), which constitutes providing a near-submission-ready answer. The part (b) discussion was handled well with genuine guided discovery. Scaffolding, dialogue quality, and communication were otherwise strong throughout.
grade.judge_reasoning: The primary deduction is under 1.1.A.a: the tutor in Turn 4 suggested the specific strategy of 'padding' K7 with isolated vertices (a key reasoning step the student had not arrived at), and in Turn 6 explicitly confirmed and named the final counterexample. Together these turns provide the student with a complete, submission-ready answer for part (a) — the claim, the construction, and the verification — with the student only performing arithmetic. This triggers the full 12-point deduction under 1.1.A.a. The part (b) induction breakdown discussion (Turns 7-10) was genuinely Socratic, with the tutor asking targeted questions and letting the student reason through the edge-count argument. Scaffolding was solid throughout, building on each student response. Dialogue quality was good with no redundancy or off-topic drift. Communication was clear and appropriately concise.
